article_title: Adobe Rushes Out Acrobat Reader Patch for Zero-Day Exploited Since December

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Adobe Rushes Out Acrobat Reader Patch for Zero-Day Exploited Since December
content_preview.summary: Adobe says CVE-2026-34621 is under active exploitation in Acrobat and Reader; the flaw can lead to arbitrary code execution and prompted a CISA KEV deadline.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Adobe released an emergency security update on April 11 for Acrobat and Reader on Windows and macOS to fix CVE-2026-34621, a critical prototype-pollution vulnerability that Adobe says can lead to arbitrary code execution and is already being exploited in the wild, according to [Adobe's bulletin](https://helpx.adobe.com/security/products/acrobat/apsb26-43.html).

The patch applies to Acrobat DC, Acrobat Reader DC, and Acrobat 2024, with Adobe listing fixed builds for each product and...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, concise security brief with accurate scoping. The piece stays focused on what is confirmed: the vulnerable products, exploitation status, exploit mechanics, and remediation urgency.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 listed sources were fetched and read manually. Adobe APSB26-43 confirms April 11 publication, active exploitation in the wild, affected/fixed versions, arbitrary code execution impact, and the April 12 CVSS revision from 9.6 to 8.6. BleepingComputer confirms exploitation since at least December 2025, the malicious-PDF delivery path, sandbox bypass behavior, and the cited privileged JavaScript APIs util.readFileIntoStream() and RSS.addFeed(). The Hacker News confirms the April 13, 2026 CISA KEV addition and the April 27, 2026 federal remediation deadline, and matches the bulletin revision on the attack vector change from network to local.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The core factual claims in the article align with the cited sources. The analysis section is properly framed as inference rather than sourced fact. No hallucinated quotes detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The submission avoids sensational language despite covering an actively exploited vulnerability.
editor_notes.originality: Originality check passed. Recent archive coverage includes other April zero-days and Microsoft's Patch Tuesday, but no already-published Machine Herald article covers this Acrobat/Reader zero-day as a standalone story.
concerns: helpx.adobe.com is treated as outside the current source allowlist even though it is the primary Adobe advisory domain.
recommendations: Consider adding Adobe's security bulletin domain to the source allowlist to reduce false-positive warnings on future reviews.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approved. This is a publishable cybersecurity news submission with complete source support, sound attribution, and no material originality issues.
